FT_IC1.evidence: "EARS app captures three weeks of passive sensing data on smartphone use in a subsample of adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study for follow-up years 4, 5, and 6."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis characterizing smartphone app usage during school hours in ABCD participants using passive sensing data and descriptive statistics.

FT_IC2.evidence: Supplementary materials show only eMethods and eReferences; no quantitative estimates linking smartphone/SM variables to individual-trait outcomes reported. Paper characterizes app usage categories (social, communication, games, etc.) during school hours but reports no associations with behavioral or health outcomes.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Paper reports app usage characterization without outcome associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: "This study analyzed prospective data from 5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018–2020, ages 11–12) and Year 4 (2020–2022, ages 13–14)."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using Poisson and linear regression models examining associations between screen time and BMI outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Obesity journal.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is multi-device aggregated ("total screen use weighted average...watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat") without separate phone/SM breakout. Per criteria, multi-device aggregated time fails IC2 unless per-device breakout exists. The composite includes TV and unspecified gaming (non-phone items) without isolation.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Screen time measure bundles TV, streaming, generic gaming, phone/SM without breakout.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed 7407 adolescents (51.6% male, age: 12.9±0.6 years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (year 3: 2019–2021 to year 5: 2021–2023)."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in World Journal of Pediatrics.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures video game behaviors (VGAQ problematic use, mature-rated game frequency, weekend gaming time) as DVs. Gaming alone without explicit phone/SM modality fails IC2. Per criteria: "Gaming alone does not qualify unless it is explicitly mobile/phone-based, OR the paper also separately measures a qualifying phone/SM variable with its own coefficient...all fail IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable." No separately-measured phone/SM IV is reported.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Outcomes are video gaming behaviors only; gaming alone without phone/SM pairing fails IC2 per binding clarification.
